Renewed Notice Of Motions And Motions By (1) Rene Leblanc And (2) Patricia Leblanc To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
Off Calendar May-09-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. LEONARD EMMA, Esq. of Emma Law, P.C. (tel: 415-362-1111) for Plaintiff (via Zoom videoconference).
Atty. ANTHONY GILES, Esq. of Law Office of Anthony Giles (tel: 415-839-2099) for Defendants Rene LeBlanc and Patricia LeBlanc.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT RENE LEBLANC AND PATRICIA LEBLANC's Motions By (1) Rene Leblanc And (2) Patricia Leblanc To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court states objections are preserved for appeal. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The "motions by (1) Rene LeBlanc and (2) Patricia LeBlanc to quash service of summons for lack of personal jurisdiction" are denied.

Plaintiff (a California resident) pleads that Texas-resident LeBlancs defrauded him in a Mexican land deal along with Patricia LeBlanc's mother (the putative landowner) and a contractor who was to build a house for plaintiff on the property (both Mexico residents).

Plaintiff declares he "exchanged approximately 75 text messages" and "multiple telephone calls" with Patricia regarding the transaction while at his California home. (Hoffman Dec. 2:18-3:5.) Plaintiff also declares that the Mexican contractor-defendant "made multiple trips to San Francisco to convince me to send money to the LeBlancs" from California to Texas, the contractor saying he was in regular communication with the LeBlancs. (Id. at 3:6-9.) Two FedEx packages plaintiff sent to the LeBlancs bore his California address and contained cashier's checks drawn on California banks payable to Rene. (Id. at 3:12-16, 25-28.)
These California contacts suffice to establish specific jurisdiction over the LeBlancs. (Sibley v. Sup. Ct. (1976) 16 Cal.3d 442, 445 [California courts "to exercise the broadest possible jurisdiction, limited only by constitutional considerations"].) This is particularly so in fraud cases. (Northern Natural Gas Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1976) 64 Cal.App.3d 983, 995 ["Where charges of fraud on California citizens are involved, jurisdictional principles should be liberally construed"]; Taylor-Rush v. Multitech Corp. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 103, 117-18 [to same effect].)

Order signed.
